content.full_text:
February 1, 1977

Mr. Steve Knoblock  
515 North Sycamore  
Santa Ana, CA 92702

Dear Mr. Knoblock:

This letter is to confirm our conversations of the last two weeks and to clarify your reporting alternatives. As I indicated, currently there is no procedure under the Political Reform Act or pursuant to Commission regulations by which a committee may formally terminate its existence. However, the Commission staff has informally advised filers in the past that they may effectively terminate by means of a two-step process. First, the committee must eliminate all debts or obligations, and dispense with all funds. Second, the treasurer should send to the Secretary of State and each person who receives a copy of the committee's final campaign statement a letter which states that the committee has eliminated all debts or obligations, dispensed with all funds, and ceased to receive contributions or make expenditures and does not anticipate doing so in the future. The letter also should advise the recipient that future campaign statements will not be filed.

If all of these steps are not carried out the committee will remain a committee even though it may not possess any present reporting obligations. For example, if the committee in question receives no contributions and makes no expenditures after the closing date for its January, 1977, semiannual statement, it would not have to file future semiannual statements but it, nevertheless, would be required to file three campaign statements in connection with the candidate's next election.

From my conversations with the Orange County Registrar, it appears that steps sufficient to terminate the

Mr. Steve Knoblock  
Page Two  
February 1, 1977

existence of the committee have not been taken. It is my understanding that the loans in question are listed as unpaid loans. However, if the committee is to terminate, the loan debts must be forgiven and the forgiveness must be listed as contributions. Thus, if the statement is not amended the committee will remain a committee with future filing obligations.

If you have any further questions, please contact me.

Sincerely,

Ted Prim  
Staff Counsel  
Legal Division

TP:jo

cc: Ms. Jeannette Shelton  
Office of the Registrar of Voters  
P.O. Box 11298  
Santa Ana, CA 92711  

Natalie

sections.question_synthetic: What procedures must a political committee follow to formally terminate its existence under the Political Reform Act?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: A committee can effectively terminate by eliminating all debts and obligations, dispensing with all funds, and sending a letter to the Secretary of State and campaign statement recipients stating the committee has ceased operations. If these steps are not completed, the committee remains subject to future filing obligations even if currently inactive.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: No formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections in document. Extracted question and conclusion as synthetic summaries from body text. Letter is dated February 1, 1977 and addresses specific committee termination inquiry. Staff counsel signature by Ted Prim confirms FPPC response.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: What procedures must a political committee follow to formally terminate its existence under the Political Reform Act?

CONCLUSION: A committee can effectively terminate by eliminating all debts and obligations, dispensing with all funds, and sending a letter to the Secretary of State and campaign statement recipients stating the committee has ceased operations. If these steps are not completed, the committee remains subject to future filing obligations even if currently inactive.

embedding.summary: FPPC staff counsel advises on the informal two-step process for committee termination: eliminating all debts/obligations and funds, then notifying relevant parties. The letter clarifies that unpaid loans must be forgiven and listed as contributions for termination to be effective.
